summary: The Paris Agreement, adopted on December 12, 2015, aimed to limit global warming to 1.5°C or 2°C above pre-industrial levels.
Global temperatures in 2024 reached 1.45°C above the 1850–1900 baseline, nearing the critical threshold, according to the IPCC Sixth Assessment Report.
Brazil's Amazon Basin deforestation in 2024 was 11,568 km², drawing criticism from the European Union and Indian advocacy groups.
Major oil companies like Shell and ExxonMobil spent $750 million on lobbying against climate legislation between 2020 and 2024, as reported by InfluenceMap.
Renewable energy capacity globally surpassed 2 terawatts in 2024, with China leading in solar power additions and wind investments.
The Paris Agreement, formally adopted on December 12, 2015, aimed to limit global warming to 1.5°C above pre-industrial levels and no more than 2°C.
By 2024, global average temperatures reached 1.45°C, nearing the 1.5°C threshold, with CO2 emissions hitting a record 37.4 gigatons in 2023.

The European Union reduced emissions by 32% since 1990 through initiatives like the European Green Deal, while Brazil faced criticism for deforestation in the Amazon under President Luiz Inácio Lula da Silva.
Major fossil fuel companies, including Saudi Aramco, Shell, and BP, spent $750 million on lobbying against climate policies between 2020 and 2024, according to a 2025 report by InfluenceMap.

The upcoming COP30 summit in Belém, Brazil, in November 2025, is crucial for nations to submit more ambitious climate commitments by 2035, with renewable energy capacity reaching 2 terawatts in 2024, led by China and wind energy companies like Ørsted and Iberdrola.
